high_novelty_claims.claim_text: The war in Iran caused the entire hydrocarbon complex, including coal and especially LNG, to be bid higher, with the Brent/WTI spread expanding significantly before reversing toward parity.

China's decision to suspend diesel and gasoline exports only six days into the conflict signals how quickly national energy protectionism emerges during supply crises, reflecting a scramble to prioritize domestic needs.
If the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for an extended period, national protectionism will create wide regional price disparities for gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and other refined products.

The convergence of Brent and WTI toward parity is partly explained by US status as both the world's largest oil producer and a net exporter of refined products, meaning overseas price spikes incentivize US refineries to run at full capacity and increase domestic crude demand.
President Trump has not yet implemented export limits or price controls on oil and refined products, which is contributing to the market dynamics driving Brent/WTI parity.

The global economy is far more fragile than at any point since 2020, with multiple regions already in or near recession before the oil shock began — making the current disruption potentially catastrophic.
The private credit bust — already underway in the US, UK, and Europe before the oil shock — is now being compounded by the energy price shock, threatening spillover into the broader financial system.

Emerging market economies are being hit with a simultaneous dollar crunch and energy cost shock — importers are suddenly needing significantly more dollars than budgeted just days earlier, creating acute monetary stress.

Middle East oil producers are already cutting output as onshore storage fills due to tanker blockage — this supply destruction could persist well beyond any immediate resolution of the strait closure.

If oil futures backwardation begins spreading from front-month contracts further out the curve toward late 2026 contracts, it would signal markets pricing in a longer-duration supply shock rather than a transient panic — a major escalation signal.
Even prominent stock market bulls like Ed Yardeni are now materially raising the probability of a US market meltdown, signaling that recession risk is finally being broadly recognized.

Once the Strait of Hormuz reopens and oil flows normalize, prices are likely to crash back to the low-to-mid $60s or lower — the current price spike has no fundamental justification and will reverse, but lasting economic damage will already have been done.
